current_hook: statue and fountain to the loyal Skye terrier
current_w: The loyal dog who guarded his master's grave for 14 years — or did he? The bronze statue at George IV Bridge, sculpted by William Brodie in 1873, has its nose rubbed gold by tourists daily; the council spent £400 restoring it to black in 2013 and it was shiny again within 48 hours. Locals ran a campaign: "Stop People Rubbing Greyfriars Bobby's Nose. It is not a Tradition." Historian Jan Bondeson reckons the whole thing was a Victorian tourist scam — cemetery curator and restaurateur keeping a stray fed to draw paying visitors, the original dog quietly swapped for a younger one. A graveyard stray, mythologised.
hook: A life-size bronze of a terrier atop a drinking fountain — and Edinburgh's smallest listed building, its nose worn gold by tourists against the council's wishes.
facts: Bronze statue of a Skye terrier on a granite drinking fountain at the southern end of George IV Bridge, near Greyfriars Kirkyard
Sculpted from life by Edinburgh sculptor William Brodie (1815–1881); unveiled without ceremony in November 1873, nearly two years after the dog's death
Commissioned and paid for by the philanthropist Baroness Angela Burdett-Coutts, an animal-welfare campaigner and president of the RSPCA Ladies' Committee
Category A listed building (listed 29 April 1977), often described as the smallest listed structure in Edinburgh
The water supply to the fountain was cut off in 1957
Tourists rub the statue's nose for luck, wearing the bronze gold; the City of Edinburgh Council discourages the practice as damaging and locals have campaigned against it
legend: Bobby is said to have guarded the grave of his owner, night-watchman John Gray, in Greyfriars Kirkyard for 14 years until his own death in 1872

Historian Jan Bondeson has argued the loyal-dog tale was largely a Victorian publicity invention, with a kirkyard stray fed to draw paying visitors and possibly more than one dog passed off as the same Bobby — this is a contested theory, not established fact

sources.note: Sculptor William Brodie, 1873 unveiling, Burdett-Coutts commission, Category A listing, smallest listed building, water cut off 1957, nose-rubbing
Background on the Bobby story, John Gray, 14-year vigil and 1872 death

notes: Jan Bondeson's debunking is well-known but is an interpretive theory; framed under legend. 'Smallest listed building' is widely repeated including on Wikipedia.

current_hook: Mons Meg, the castle's giant medieval siege gun
current_w: Mons Meg is a medieval bombard in the collection of the Royal Armouries, on loan to Historic Environment Scotland and located at Edinburgh Castle in Scotland. It has a barrel diameter of 20 inches (510 mm), making it one of the largest cannons in the world by calibre.
hook: A 6-tonne 15th-century siege bombard with a 20-inch bore — once able to hurl a 330-pound stone ball two miles, until its own barrel burst firing a salute in 1680.
facts: Medieval bombard with a calibre (bore) of about 20 inches (510 mm), among the largest-calibre cannon in the world
Built around 1449 in Mons, in the County of Hainaut (modern Belgium), by Burgundian gunmaker Jehan Cambier at the request of Duke Philip the Good
Made of longitudinal iron staves bound by iron hoops; weighs roughly 15,366 lb (about 6,970 kg) and is about 15 ft (4.6 m) long
Presented to King James II of Scotland in 1454
Could fire stone shot of roughly 330 lb (about 150 kg)
Its barrel burst in 1680, reportedly while firing a salute; taken to the Tower of London in 1754 and returned to Edinburgh Castle in 1829 after a campaign by Sir Walter Scott

sources.note: Calibre, weight, length, 1449 construction by Jehan Cambier in Mons, 1454 gift to James II, 1680 burst, 1754 removal and 1829 return
Historic Environment Scotland collections record: Jehan Cambier of Mons, 1449, late medieval

notes: Owned by the Royal Armouries, displayed at Edinburgh Castle in HES care. Two-mile range figure is widely cited; bore/weight/dates are well documented.

current_hook: the Heart of Midlothian pavement mosaic
current_w: The Heart of Midlothian is a heart-shaped mosaic, formed of coloured granite setts, located outside St Giles' Cathedral in Edinburgh. It marks the site of the entrance to Edinburgh's Old Tolbooth, which was demolished in 1817.
hook: A heart picked out in the cobbles outside St Giles' that Edinburghers still spit on — a gesture of contempt for the prison and gallows that once stood on the spot.
facts: Heart-shaped mosaic of coloured granite setts set into the pavement on the Royal Mile, just outside St Giles' Cathedral
Marks the site of the entrance to the Old Tolbooth, Edinburgh's administrative centre, prison and place of public execution
The Tolbooth stood from the 15th century until it was demolished in 1817
The name comes from Sir Walter Scott's 1818 novel 'The Heart of Midlothian', published within about a year of the building's demolition
Locals traditionally spit on the heart; the gesture originally signalled disdain for the prison and its conditions (and for tax collectors), though today it is often done for luck
legend: The spitting custom is sometimes linked to the 1736 Porteous Riots, when Captain John Porteous of the Town Guard was seized near the Tolbooth and lynched in the Grassmarket — a popular association rather than a documented origin of the tradition

sources.note: Granite-sett mosaic outside St Giles, marks Old Tolbooth entrance, demolished 1817, Scott novel, spitting tradition and its meaning
Scotsman explainer on the spitting tradition, Tolbooth prison/execution history and Porteous association

notes: Tolbooth dated 15th–19th century; demolition 1817 confirmed. Porteous-riot link is folkloric and framed under legend.

current_w: Architectural structure in City of Edinburgh, Scotland, UK. Scheduled monument.
hook: A roughly 2.75-metre prehistoric monolith, said to be over 5,000 years old, now standing in a recess in a suburban garden wall in southern Edinburgh.
facts: Prehistoric standing stone (menhir) of red sandstone, set in a recess in a garden wall on Caiystane View near Oxgangs Road, Fairmilehead/Oxgangs, southern Edinburgh
Stands about 9 ft 3 in (roughly 2.75 m) tall, with breadth up to about 1.55 m
Carries a row of about six weathered cup-marks low on one face, consistent with a Neolithic/Bronze Age date; reputed to be over 5,000 years old
Surrounding area once held cairns, cists and urns, suggesting Bronze Age burial/ritual use of the hilltop
Owned by the National Trust for Scotland, to which it was given in 1936
Known over the centuries by several names, including Cat Stane, Cetstane, Kel Stane and Camus Stone

sources.note: Dimensions, red sandstone, six cup-marks, alternative names, surrounding cairns/cists
National Trust for Scotland ownership, given to the Trust in 1936, 'over 5000 years old' NTS plaque

notes: 'Over 5000 years old' is the NTS plaque figure; cup-marks point to Neolithic/Bronze Age. Scheduled monument and NTS-owned both confirmed.

current_hook: well marking the site of witch burnings
current_w: The Witches' Well is a monument to accused witches burned at the stake in Edinburgh, Scotland, and is the only one of its kind in the city.
hook: A cast-iron fountain on the Castle Esplanade marking the spot where more accused witches were burned than anywhere else in Scotland
facts: Commissioned by town planner and biologist Sir Patrick Geddes in 1894 and designed by his friend, the artist John Duncan
Set into a wall at the lower end of Edinburgh Castle Esplanade, near where accused witches were strangled and burned
The relief shows a serpent coiled around the heads of Hygeia (Greek goddess of health) and her father Aesculapius (god of medicine), plus a foxglove plant
Roman numerals mark the dates 1479 and 1722, bracketing the most intense period of witch persecution in Scotland
A descriptive plaque was added in 1912; historians have criticised its wording for implying the victims had real magical powers
The serpent and foxglove are deliberately dual-meaning symbols of both evil and wisdom / healing, reflecting how the accused were seen

sources.note: Commission by Geddes 1894, design by John Duncan, symbolism, 1479/1722 dates, 1912 plaque
Memorial drinking fountain details and esplanade location

notes: Sometimes called a fountain/drinking fountain; spout now permanently dry. It is a memorial to the accused, not the only such monument in Scotland (Edinburgh-specific).

current_w: Public sculpture in Edinburgh. Work by Antony Gormley. Built in 2010.
hook: Six iron casts of Antony Gormley's own body trail down the Water of Leith from the modern-art gallery to the sea, with four standing in the river as flood gauges
facts: A series of six life-size cast-iron figures by Turner Prize-winning sculptor Antony Gormley (born 1950), each a cast of the artist's own body
The figures run between the grounds of the Scottish National Gallery of Modern Art and the sea at Leith Docks, following the Water of Leith
Each figure measures roughly 191 x 50 x 36 cm and looks in a different direction, reflected in the subtitles Horizon, Ground, Left, Right and Sky
Four of the figures stand in the river itself, acting as gauges that show the water's height as it rises and falls
Commissioned by the Scottish National Gallery of Modern Art with Art Fund support; installed in 2010 and reinstalled in 2019
In 2010 some of the river figures were knocked over / submerged by floodwater shortly after installation

sources.note: Artist's own page: six figures, gallery-to-sea siting, cast iron
National Galleries of Scotland: commission, 2010 install / 2019 reinstall, figures as river gauges

notes: Title is '6 TIMES'. Subtitles listed as Horizon, Ground, Left, Right, Sky (six figures total).

current_w: Historic tomb in Edinburgh. A heritage-listed structure. Category C listed building.
hook: A lone 1645 plague tomb, carved with a grinning skull, marooned in a tenement back garden off Chamberlain Road
facts: Marks the grave of John Livingston, an Edinburgh apothecary who died in the 1645 plague outbreak, aged about 53
He was buried on his own estate at Greenhill, then open country on the Burgh Muir well beyond the city, rather than in a city churchyard
The tomb bears a Latin epitaph 'Mors patet; Hora latet' — 'Death is sure; the hour obscure'
The stone is carved with a grinning skull and crossbones, typical memento mori of the period
It survives as one of Edinburgh's smallest and most unusual burial grounds, now beside 1 Chamberlain Road
A 2004 dispute confirmed the plot must remain publicly accessible and maintained by the council
legend: He is said to have caught the plague from people evacuated/quarantined out of the city onto the Burgh Muir, and the custom of burying plague victims where they died is offered as why he lies alone here (framed as tradition rather than documented fact)

sources.note: Apothecary, 1645 plague death aged ~53, epitaph, skull carving, Burgh Muir burial, 2004 access dispute
Context of the 1645 Edinburgh plague and Burgh Muir / Bruntsfield burials

notes: Some sources name his wife Elizabeth Rig as also dying in 1645 and being commemorated; the listed-building category (C) is in the current_w but I could not independently confirm the exact HES listing category in this pass.

current_w: Cannon in City of Edinburgh, Scotland, UK.
hook: A bronze cannon cast in Iberia that crossed three empires and four centuries before a British war prize landed it beside the Dugald Stewart Monument
facts: A bronze (brass) cannon standing on Calton Hill, near the Dugald Stewart Monument
It bears a Spanish royal coat of arms — Spain ruled Portugal at the time it was cast — and a separate Burmese inscription
In the 17th century it was sent out for service in the Portuguese Indies, the trade empire stretching from Mozambique to Macao
It later passed to the rulers of Arakan (the west coast of modern Myanmar) and, per its Burmese inscription, was taken to Mandalay in 1785
British forces captured it when Upper Burma fell in the Third Anglo-Burmese War of 1885
It was presented to Edinburgh in 1886 and placed on Calton Hill the following year

sources.note: Spanish arms, Portuguese Indies, Arakan, 1785 Mandalay, 1885 capture, 1886 presentation, Calton Hill / Dugald Stewart Monument
Calton Hill Portuguese Cannon monument overview

notes: Despite the name it carries a Spanish royal crest (Spain ruled Portugal 1580-1640). Exact casting date/weight/calibre not confirmed in this pass.
